one_line_takeaway: EditMark shows model editing (not fine-tuning or backdoors) can embed high-capacity (32/64/128-bit), stealthy, near-performance-lossless LLM ownership watermarks in seconds via an adaptive multi-round noise-hardened editing strategy, achieving 100% extraction success across five open LLMs, but the scheme is significantly weakened when an attacker has partial knowledge of the watermark's question template (ESR as low as 3%).
key_finding: EditMark embeds a 32-bit watermark in under 20 seconds per model (9.47-12.9s across the five base models) with 100% ESR, versus KIMark's 5,563-9,003s (0-shot 100% or 99.1% ESR) and the fine-tuning baseline cited as averaging 6,875 seconds -- i.e., editing is reported as roughly 300x faster than fine-tuning while matching or beating ESR. Backdoor watermarking is fast-to-moderate (3,386-5,198s) but has poor and highly variable ESR (2.2%-83.5% at only 1-bit capacity). BadEdit is fast (37-150s) but only 1-bit capacity with 88.5%-99.7% ESR, below EditMark's 100%. At larger capacities EditMark still achieves 100% ESR for 64-bit and 128-bit watermarks with embedding time under 2 minutes. Fidelity is largely preserved: average benchmark scores shift by well under 1 point (e.g., MMLU average 44.1% original -> 44.4% at 32-bit and 128-bit; BLiMP 79.8%->79.9%). Stealthiness: EditMark's watermarked-text perplexity (e.g., 27.01 on LLaMA-3-8B vs. natural-text PPL of 23.6-34.09) is much closer to natural text than BadEdit's (137.05 on LLaMA-3-8B), evidencing more natural/logical watermarked outputs. Robustness varies by attack: watermark survives Int-8 quantization fully (100% ESR) but Int-4 degrades ESR while also hurting model performance; noise/pruning attacks can reduce ESR but only by simultaneously degrading model utility substantially (e.g., a 31.3% BLiMP performance drop on Baichuan-7B under noise strong enough to meaningfully erode the watermark); model-editing removal attacks (CounterFact-based, same-layer, up to 50 edits) leave ESR above 95% for all models; but adaptive/overwrite attacks are the clear weak point -- when the attacker knows the exact MA question template except the subject (Scenario A), ESR collapses to as low as 3%-40% across models (e.g., Baichuan-7B: 3.0%), and even under the more realistic no-knowledge Scenario B it can drop to 4% (Baichuan-7B) though other models retain 33%-74%. Ablations show both the multi-round stable editing strategy and the Gaussian noise-matrix injection are necessary: without stable editing, noise injection alone can trigger gradient explosion and editing failure (e.g., GPT-J-6B, GPT2-XL); single-shot AlphaEdit editing without the paper's multi-round strategy fails badly on some models/capacities (e.g., GPT-J-6B: 0% ESR at 32-bit and 64-bit, 2.5% at 128-bit).
core_contribution: The paper proposes EditMark, a training-free LLM watermarking framework that embeds ownership watermarks into open-source LLM weights via model editing rather than fine-tuning or backdoor insertion. It exploits 'multiple-answer' (MA) questions (e.g., integer-solution math inequalities that admit many valid answers) so that a specific permutation of the answer encodes watermark bits; a Generator builds MA questions from templates using a PRNG-seeded integer sequence, an Encoder maps the watermark bits to a target answer permutation via a lexicographic-permutation encoding/decoding scheme (with a formal correctness proof, Theorem 1), and an Editor performs the actual weight edit. The paper's own technical contribution beyond using off-the-shelf model editing (AlphaEdit) is an 'adaptive multi-round stable editing strategy' that iteratively refines the edit until an editing-score residual falls below a threshold (with gradient-explosion-preventing regularization of the perturbation norm), plus injection of Gaussian noise into the edit's key representations (inspired by the noise layer used in deep image watermarking) to harden the watermark against downstream noise/pruning attacks. A Decoder later queries the watermarked model with the same MA questions and inverts the permutation back to watermark bits.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section IV-A. Two parties: model owner and attacker. Watermark embedding happens under a white-box setting (the owner has full weight access before releasing/selling the model). Watermark extraction happens under a black-box setting: the owner cannot access weights/parameters, cannot observe internal features or logits, and cannot control inference parameters (temperature, top-p) of the suspect model. The attacker is assumed aware that a watermark exists, has white-box access to the (stolen/resold) model (can fine-tune, prune, quantize it), and can control inference parameters. The paper's motivating scenario is unauthorized resale/redeployment of an open-source LLM as an API service, with the goal of tracing/proving provenance -- an IP-protection/copyright threat model, not a jailbreak, prompt-injection, or data-poisoning threat model.
limitations: The paper's own experiments surface several weaknesses rather than a dedicated limitations section (the crawled 22-page excerpt does not contain a distinct Limitations/Conclusion section, so this is drawn from result discussion). (1) Adaptive/overwrite attacks by an attacker with partial knowledge of the MA question template are the method's most serious vulnerability, driving ESR down to single digits for some models (Baichuan-7B: 3-4%). (2) Robustness against noise and pruning attacks is only relative -- the watermark is not attack-proof, it merely forces the attacker to also degrade model performance to remove it (a 'stealthiness/robustness via cost to the attacker' argument, not an information-theoretic guarantee). (3) Int-4 quantization does meaningfully erode ESR (only partial robustness claimed, 'certain degree of robustness'). (4) Robustness against LoRA fine-tuning weakens with more epochs on some models (Qwen-7B shows a noticeable ESR decline by epoch 3). (5) The MA-question approach relies on domain-specific templates (math inequalities) generated with GPT-4o assistance to avoid 'edit entanglement' among semantically similar questions -- generality to other knowledge domains/question types is not demonstrated. (6) Editing is restricted to a single MLP layer per model, and the threat model explicitly assumes the model-editing attacker does not initially know which layer holds the watermark; if that assumption fails (as tested directly) robustness still holds reasonably well, but the adaptive-attack results show template knowledge is a more damaging leak than layer knowledge.

datasets_used: C4 (allenai/c4, for LoRA fine-tuning attack subset of 2000 texts, and as a natural-text source for PPL stealthiness comparison)
AGNews (fancyzhx/ag_news, natural-text source for PPL stealthiness comparison)
GPT-4o-generated MA question templates (mathematical inequality templates used to construct watermark carrier questions)
quantitative_results: EditMark: 100% ESR for 32-bit watermark across all 5 models, embedding time 9.47s-12.9s
EditMark: 100% ESR at 64-bit and 128-bit capacities, embedding time under 2 minutes
Fine-tuning cited average embedding cost: 6875 seconds (EditMark >300x faster)
Fidelity: average MMLU 44.1% (original) vs 44.4% (32-bit and 128-bit watermarked)
Stealthiness PPL (LLaMA-3-8B): EditMark 27.01 vs BadEdit 137.05 vs natural text 23.6-34.09
Model-editing removal attack (CounterFact, up to 50 edits): ESR stays >=95% for all 5 models
Adaptive attack Scenario A (attacker knows template minus subject): ESR drops to 40% (GPT2-XL), 26% (GPT-J-6B), 10% (LLaMA-3-8B), 3% (Baichuan-7B), 29% (Qwen-7B)
Adaptive attack Scenario B (no template knowledge): ESR 52% (GPT2-XL), 44% (GPT-J-6B), 33% (LLaMA-3-8B), 4% (Baichuan-7B), 74% (Qwen-7B)
Noise attack: Baichuan-7B shows 31.3% BLiMP performance drop when noise strong enough to degrade ESR; Qwen-7B 5.3% GLUE decline; LLaMA-3-8B 11.2% MMLU decrease
Pruning attack: Baichuan-7B 27.3% BLiMP drop; Qwen-7B 4.8% GLUE decline; LLaMA-3-8B 5.0% MMLU decrease at ESR-degrading pruning ratios
Ablation (editing strategy): original single-shot AlphaEdit vs EditMark's multi-round strategy on GPT-J-6B: 0% vs 100% ESR at 32-bit, 0% vs 100% at 64-bit, 2.5% vs 100% at 128-bit
Quantization: Int-8 leaves ESR at 100% for all models; Int-4 partially reduces ESR but also significantly degrades model performance
Model size ablation (Qwen-14B, RTX A6000): 100% average ESR at 32/64-bit capacities
baselines_compared: BadEdit (backdoor-injection method via model editing, repurposed as a watermarking baseline)
